title: weekly-sync-feb17
content:
# Team Weekly Sync - February 17, 2026

## Attendees
- John (Product Manager)
- Emily (Engineering Lead)
- David (Designer)
- Rachel (QA Lead)

---

[00:00] **John**: Alright everyone, let's start our weekly sync. How's everyone doing today?

[00:15] **Emily**: I'm good. We had a busy week with the backend migration.

[00:22] **John**: Right, Emily, can you give us an update on the migration?

[00:28] **Emily**: Sure. We finished moving 80% of user data to the new cluster. The remaining 20% will be done by Wednesday. We've also implemented automatic failover, so if the primary goes down, it switches to the backup within 30 seconds.

[00:45] **David**: That's great progress. How's the performance looking?

[00:50] **Emily**: Actually, latency has improved by about 40% on average. Users in Europe are seeing response times around 120ms now, down from 200ms before.

[01:05] **Rachel**: That's awesome. I've been monitoring the error rates during the migration. We've seen a slight increase in timeout errors - about 2% - but nothing critical. Most of those are retry-able.

[01:18] **John**: Good to hear. David, what about the new dashboard design?

[01:24] **David**: I've completed the wireframes. The new layout puts the key metrics front and center. I'm now working on the high-fidelity mockups. Should have those ready for review by Thursday.

[01:38] **John**: Perfect. Are we still on track for the beta launch on March 1st?

[01:44] **Emily**: I think so. The core features are done. We're mainly working on bug fixes and performance tuning now.

[01:52] **Rachel**: I've started drafting the test cases for the beta. I'll need everyone to review the acceptance criteria by Friday so we can finalize the test plan.

[02:05] **John**: Absolutely. Emily, can you send around the API change log? David, please share the design specs. Rachel, send the test plan template.

[02:15] **Emily**: Will do.

[02:18] **David**: Already in the shared folder.

[02:22] **Rachel**: I'll have it out by end of day.

[02:28] **John**: Great. Any blockers we need to discuss?

[02:32] **Emily**: Actually, yes. We need to decide on the caching strategy. The current Redis setup is working, but I'm not sure if we should scale it vertically or horizontally.

[02:42] **John**: What's the difference in effort?

[02:46] **Emily**: Vertical scaling is easier - just add more memory to the instance. Horizontal would require sharding, which is more complex but better for long-term. I'd recommend going horizontal since we're expecting significant growth.

[02:58] **David**: I agree. We should plan for scale now rather than deal with it later.

[03:05] **John**: Rachel, any concerns from QA perspective?

[03:10] **Rachel**: The main thing is we need clear documentation on the new caching behavior so we can test it properly. Especially around cache invalidation scenarios.

[03:20] **Emily**: I'll write up a detailed doc on that.

[03:25] **John**: Okay, so decision made: we go with horizontal scaling for Redis. Emily to document the caching strategy. Let's also schedule a deeper dive on the caching implementation next week.

[03:35] **David**: Sounds good.

[03:38] **Rachel**: Agreed.

[03:42] **John**: Anything else? If not, let's wrap up. Remember, design review Thursday, test plan review Friday. Thanks everyone!

[03:52] **All**: Thanks, John. Have a good week!

outline: Attendees: John (PM), Emily (Engineering Lead), David (Designer), Rachel (QA Lead)
80% user data migrated to new cluster, remaining 20% by Wednesday
Automatic failover implemented (30-second switch to backup)
Performance improvements: 40% latency reduction, European users at 120ms (down from 200ms)
Error monitoring: 2% increase in timeout errors (non-critical, retry-able)
Wireframes completed, high-fidelity mockups in progress
Target: ready for review by Thursday
Core features complete, focus on bug fixes and performance tuning
Test cases drafting in progress, acceptance criteria review needed by Friday
David: share design specs (already in shared folder)
Rachel: send test plan template by end of day
Decision made: horizontal scaling for Redis (better for long-term growth)
Emily to document caching strategy with details on cache invalidation
Schedule deeper dive on caching implementation next week
QA Requirements: documentation on new caching behavior for proper testing
Closing: design review Thursday, test plan review Friday

action_items.task: Send API change log to team
Share design specs (already completed)
Send test plan template by end of day
Document caching strategy (including cache invalidation scenarios)
Review acceptance criteria by Friday
Schedule deeper dive session on caching implementation for next week

knowledge_points: Backend migration 80% complete, remaining 20% due by Wednesday
Automatic failover reduces downtime to 30 seconds
Latency improved 40% overall, European users see 120ms response times
Timeout errors increased by 2% but are non-critical and retry-able
Horizontal Redis scaling chosen for long-term scalability despite higher complexity
QA needs clear caching behavior documentation, especially for invalidation scenarios
Beta launch targeted for March 1st with core features complete

title: marketing-strategy-feb10
content:
# Marketing Strategy Meeting - February 10, 2026

## Participants
- Jennifer (CMO)
- Mark (Marketing Director)
- Sophie (Content Lead)
- Tom (Growth Lead)

---

[00:00] **Jennifer**: Good morning everyone. Thanks for making time for this. Today we're going to discuss our Q2 marketing strategy and budget allocation.

[00:15] **Mark**: I've prepared a deck with some initial proposals. Let me share my screen.

[00:22] **Jennifer**: Go ahead.

[00:25] **Mark**: So looking at our current numbers, we're spending about 60% of our budget on paid ads, 25% on content, and 15% on events. I think we need to rebalance this.

[00:38] **Sophie**: I'm biased, but I think content deserves more. Our organic traffic has been growing 20% month over month, and the quality of leads from blog posts is much better than paid.

[00:48] **Tom**: I see it differently. Our paid campaigns have a clear ROI. We can measure exactly how much revenue each dollar generates. Content is harder to measure.

[00:58] **Sophie**: That's a fair point, but we're also building a brand asset that compounds over time. It's not just about immediate conversion.

[01:10] **Jennifer**: Both valid points. Mark, what does the data say about our customer acquisition cost by channel?

[01:18] **Mark**: So paid social is around $45 per acquisition. Content marketing is about $28. Email is the cheapest at $12, but our list isn't growing fast enough.

[01:28] **Tom**: Right, that's why I'm pushing for more investment in paid. We can scale what works.

[01:35] **Sophie**: But we're reaching saturation on Facebook and Instagram. The CPMs keep going up. We should invest in new channels like LinkedIn for B2B or TikTok for awareness.

[01:48] **Mark**: Sophie has a point. Our CPM on LinkedIn is half of what we pay on Meta. And the leads from LinkedIn convert at a higher rate.

[01:58] **Jennifer**: Okay, here's what I'm thinking. Let's shift 10% of our budget from paid social to LinkedIn. Sophie, can you create a content calendar for LinkedIn?

[02:08] **Sophie**: Definitely. I can repurpose some of our blog content and create native posts.

[02:15] **Tom**: I'm concerned about bandwidth. Who will manage the LinkedIn presence?

[02:22] **Sophie**: I can handle it with my team. We might need to hire a contractor for additional support, but let's see how it goes for a month first.

[02:32] **Jennifer**: Sounds reasonable. Mark, what about events? We had three conferences planned.

[02:40] **Mark**: I recommend we keep the enterprise conference in March - that's our biggest lead generator. But let's cancel the two smaller regional events. We can reinvest that money into digital.

[02:52] **Jennifer**: Any objections?

[03:00] **Tom**: No, that makes sense. Those events were expensive for the ROI we were getting.

[03:08] **Sophie**: Agreed.

[03:10] **Jennifer**: Great. So final proposal: reduce paid social by 10%, reallocate to LinkedIn, cancel two regional events. Content gets a slight bump. Let me know if I missed anything.

[03:22] **Mark**: One more thing - we need to talk about the product launch in April. We should start the buzz campaign three weeks before.

[03:30] **Jennifer**: Good call. Tom, can you put together a campaign brief?

[03:36] **Tom**: Will do. I'll have it ready by Friday.

[03:42] **Jennifer**: Perfect. Let's summarize action items. Mark - finalize budget reallocation by end of week. Sophie - LinkedIn content calendar by next Monday. Tom - campaign brief by Friday. Jennifer - review and approve. Any questions?

[03:55] **Mark**: No, looks good.

[03:58] **Sophie**: All clear.

[04:02] **Tom**: Got it.

[04:05] **Jennifer**: Excellent meeting everyone. Let's reconvene next week to review progress.

outline: Q2 Marketing Strategy and Budget Allocation
- Paid campaigns: clear ROI, measurable revenue
- Content marketing: builds compounding brand asset, lower CAC
- Email: $12 per acquisition (but list growth slow)
- LinkedIn: half the CPM of Meta, higher conversion rate
- Recommendation: invest in LinkedIn for B2B, TikTok for awareness
- Shift 10% budget from paid social to LinkedIn
- Cancel two smaller regional events, keep enterprise conference (March)
- Need buzz campaign three weeks before launch

action_items.task: Finalize budget reallocation
Create LinkedIn content calendar
Put together product launch campaign brief
Review and approve budget and campaign

knowledge_points: Paid social CAC: $45, Content marketing CAC: $28, Email CAC: $12
LinkedIn CPM is half of Meta's CPM
LinkedIn leads convert at higher rate than other paid social channels
Organic traffic growing 20% month over month
Enterprise conference is the team's biggest lead generator
Facebook and Instagram ads reaching saturation with increasing CPMs
Product launch in April requires buzz campaign starting 3 weeks prior
Sophie can repurpose blog content for LinkedIn native posts

title: client-demo-jan28
content:
# Client Demo - Acme Corp - January 28, 2026

## Present
- Kevin (Sales)
- Nina (Solutions Engineer)
- Robert (Acme VP of Engineering)
- Patricia (Acme Product Manager)

---

[00:00] **Kevin**: Hi everyone, thank you for taking the time today. I'll kick things off and then Nina will walk you through the demo.

[00:10] **Robert**: Thanks for scheduling this. We're evaluating three vendors, so we have about an hour.

[00:18] **Kevin**: Perfect. Let's make the most of it. Before we start, can you give us a quick refresher on what you're looking to solve?

[00:25] **Robert**: We're dealing with massive data silos. Our marketing team uses one platform, sales uses another, and customer support has their own system. We need everything connected.

[00:35] **Patricia**: Specifically, we want to understand the customer journey from first touch to renewal. Right now, we can't track that reliably.

[00:45] **Kevin**: That makes sense. Nina, do you want to start the demo?

[00:52] **Nina**: Absolutely. Let me share my screen. Here's our dashboard view. This is what your team would see after implementation. The first thing you'll notice is the unified customer profile.

[01:05] **Nina**: Every interaction across all touchpoints is aggregated here. You can see emails, calls, support tickets, usage data - all in one place.

[01:15] **Patricia**: That's clean. How does the data get unified?

[01:22] **Nina**: We use a combination of deterministic matching and probabilistic modeling. For email addresses and phone numbers, we can match with 99% accuracy. For anonymous web visitors, we use behavioral signals to create probabilistic profiles.

[01:35] **Robert**: What about data privacy? GDPR, CCPA compliance?

[01:42] **Nina**: Great question. We have built-in consent management. You can configure data retention policies per region. There's also a data deletion API that integrates with right-to-be-forgotten requests.

[01:55] **Patricia**: How long does implementation typically take?

[02:02] **Nina**: For a company your size, we'd typically go live in 8 to 12 weeks. Phase one is data integration - that's about 4 weeks. Phase two is dashboard customization - another 4 weeks. Then testing and rollout.

[02:15] **Kevin**: I should mention we have a dedicated success team that stays with you through implementation and beyond.

[02:22] **Robert**: What about API access? We want to build custom integrations.

[02:30] **Nina**: Full API access is included in the enterprise plan. We have REST and GraphQL APIs. Here's a quick example - this is a real-time webhook that pushes data to your internal systems.

[02:42] **Nina**: We also have a marketplace with over 200 pre-built connectors. Salesforce, HubSpot, Zendesk, ServiceNow - most major platforms are covered.

[02:55] **Patricia**: What about reporting? We need executive dashboards.

[03:05] **Nina**: We have a drag-and-drop report builder. You can create custom visualizations. There's also a scheduled email feature - reports can be sent automatically to stakeholders every week or month.

[03:18] **Robert**: Can you show us the mobile experience?

[03:22] **Nina**: Of course. Here's our iOS app. It has offline support, which is important for our sales team. They can view customer profiles and log activities even without internet. It syncs when they're back online.

[03:35] **Patricia**: The interface looks intuitive. One concern I have is user training. Our team is not very technical.

[03:45] **Nina**: We have extensive onboarding support. There's an in-app guided tour for new users. We also offer training sessions - both live and recorded. Plus, our support team is available 24/7.

[03:58] **Kevin**: We've had clients as large as 50,000 employees successfully onboard. The learning curve is typically about two weeks for average users.

[04:10] **Robert**: What's the pricing model?

[04:15] **Kevin**: It's based on the number of users and data volume. For your use case, we're looking at around $150,000 annually. That includes unlimited API calls and 24/7 support.

[04:25] **Robert**: That's helpful. We have a few more vendors to evaluate. What's your timeline?

[04:32] **Patricia**: We're hoping to make a decision by end of Q1. So roughly 60 days.

[04:40] **Kevin**: Plenty of time. We can do a technical deep dive next week if you want. Nina can also prepare a proof of concept with some sample data.

[04:50] **Robert**: That would be helpful.

[04:55] **Patricia**: One more question - what happens if you get acquired or go out of business? We need to know our data is safe.

[05:05] **Nina**: Totally reasonable concern. We have a data portability guarantee. You can export all your data at any time in standard formats. We also have a financial stability report we can share - we're well-funded with Series C funding.

[05:18] **Kevin**: And we can include data escrow in the contract as an additional safeguard.

[05:25] **Robert**: Good to know. I think we have what we need for today. Thanks Nina, Kevin.

[05:32] **Kevin**: Thank you both for your time. I'll follow up tomorrow with the technical deep dive scheduling. Have a great day!

[05:40] **Patricia**: Thanks, you too.

[05:43] **Robert**: Talk soon.

outline: - Robert mentions they are evaluating 3 vendors with about an hour
- Robert explains data silos issue: marketing, sales, customer support use different platforms
- Patricia wants to track customer journey from first touch to renewal
- Nina demonstrates dashboard with unified customer profile
- Aggregates emails, calls, support tickets, usage data
- Probabilistic modeling for anonymous web visitors
- Configurable data retention policies per region
- Data deletion API for right-to-be-forgotten requests
- Full API access in enterprise plan (REST and GraphQL)
- Real-time webhook for pushing data to internal systems
- Marketplace with 200+ pre-built connectors (Salesforce, HubSpot, Zendesk, ServiceNow)
- View customer profiles and log activities without internet
- In-app guided tour for new users
- Learning curve typically ~2 weeks for average users
- Based on number of users and data volume
- Includes unlimited API calls and 24/7 support
- Acme aims to decide by end of Q1 (~60 days)
- Kevin proposes technical deep dive for next week
- Nina to prepare proof of concept with sample data
- Patricia asks about data safety if vendor is acquired/goes out of business
- Nina explains data portability guarantee (export in standard formats)
- Financial stability: Series C funding, well-funded
- Kevin offers data escrow in contract as additional safeguard
- Robert expresses they have what they need for today

action_items.task: Schedule technical deep dive for next week
Prepare proof of concept with sample data
Follow up tomorrow with technical deep dive scheduling

knowledge_points: Acme Corp has data silos across marketing, sales, and customer support platforms
Solution provides unified customer profile tracking entire customer journey
Data matching uses deterministic matching (99% accuracy for emails/phones) and probabilistic modeling for anonymous visitors
Platform is GDPR and CCPA compliant with built-in consent management and configurable data retention
Implementation timeline is 8-12 weeks: 4 weeks data integration, 4 weeks dashboard customization, plus testing
Enterprise plan includes full REST and GraphQL API access with 200+ pre-built connectors
Reporting includes drag-and-drop builder with scheduled email automation
iOS mobile app supports offline viewing with auto-sync
User onboarding takes ~2 weeks for average users; platform has in-app tours, live/recorded training, 24/7 support
Pricing is ~$150,000 annually based on users and data volume, includes unlimited API calls and 24/7 support
Acme plans to make vendor decision by end of Q1 (approximately 60 days)
Data portability guarantee allows export in standard formats; vendor is Series C funded with option for data escrow

title: auth-architecture-feb18
content:
# Technical Architecture Review - February 18, 2026

## Present
- Alex (Principal Engineer)
- Maria (Senior Backend Engineer)
- James (DevOps Engineer)
- Linda (Security Engineer)

---

[00:00] **Alex**: Thanks for joining. Today we need to make a decision on our authentication system. We've outgrown our current JWT implementation and need to discuss options.

[00:12] **Maria**: Let me walk through the current state first. We have about 2 million daily active users. Our current JWT tokens expire after 24 hours. We're seeing some issues with token refresh on mobile - about 5% of users experience stuck sessions.

[00:25] **James**: From the infrastructure side, the JWT validation is adding latency. We're doing 50,000 validations per minute at peak. Each validation takes about 15ms because we're hitting the database to verify the token hasn't been revoked.

[00:38] **Linda**: That brings up security concerns. If you're doing database lookups for every token validation, you're creating a bottleneck that could be exploited. We should be using a distributed cache.

[00:48] **Maria**: That's exactly why we're here. We're considering three options. Option one is to implement Redis caching with a shorter TTL. Option two is to switch to opaque tokens with a session database. Option three is to move to OAuth 2.0 with a third-party identity provider.

[01:02] **Alex**: Let's discuss pros and cons. Maria, start with option one.

[01:08] **Maria**: Option one is the quickest to implement. We keep JWTs but add Redis caching. Tokens would still be self-contained, so no database hit for valid cached tokens. We'd reduce latency to under 5ms. Downside is we still have the complexity of token refresh on the client side.

[01:22] **James**: We can definitely support option one from infrastructure. We'd need to provision a Redis cluster with high availability. I can have that ready in two weeks.

[01:32] **Linda**: I'm okay with option one from a security perspective, provided we implement proper key rotation. We need to make sure we have a process to rotate the signing keys without downtime.

[01:44] **Alex**: What's option two?

[01:48] **Maria**: Option two is to use opaque tokens stored in a database. This gives us immediate revocation capability - no waiting for cache to expire. The downside is every API call hits the database, so we'd need read replicas and possibly a caching layer anyway. It's more complex.

[01:58] **James**: That's a lot more work on our end. We're looking at three to four weeks minimum for implementation, plus ongoing maintenance.

[02:08] **Alex**: And option three?

[02:12] **Maria**: Option three is to outsource auth entirely. Use Auth0 or Okta. Pros: no maintenance, enterprise features built in, SOC2 compliance ready. Cons: costs money, we're giving up control, and migration would take time.

[02:25] **Linda**: From security standpoint, option three is the strongest. They have dedicated security teams and stay up to date with latest standards. But we need to factor in the cost and dependency.

[02:38] **Alex**: Let's talk numbers. What's the cost comparison?

[02:44] **Maria**: Option one costs us nothing extra - just Redis which we already have. Option two would require additional database infrastructure, maybe $500 per month. Option three would be around $2,000 per month for our user base.

[02:56] **James**: My vote is for option one. Fastest to implement, solves the immediate problem, and we can always migrate to option three later if needed.

[03:05] **Linda**: Agreed. Let's start simple and add complexity when we need it.

[03:12] **Alex**: Maria, what do you think?

[03:15] **Maria**: I'm comfortable with option one. The mobile session issues should be solvable with proper token refresh logic on the client side.

[03:24] **Alex**: Okay, we have consensus. Option one it is. Let me summarize the action items.

[03:30] **Alex**: James - provision Redis cluster with 99.99% SLA by end of week two. Maria - implement token caching logic and handle key rotation. Linda - update security documentation and run a penetration test after deployment. Any questions?

[03:45] **James**: One thing - we need to decide on cache TTL. What's your recommendation?

[03:52] **Maria**: I was thinking 15 minutes for the cache. That gives us a good balance between performance and security.

[03:58] **Linda**: 15 minutes is reasonable. Make sure to implement cache invalidation for the revoke endpoint though.

[04:05] **Maria**: Absolutely, I'll include that.

[04:10] **Alex**: Great. Let's also schedule a follow-up in two weeks to review progress. Thanks everyone.

outline: Present: Alex (Principal Engineer), Maria (Senior Backend Engineer), James (DevOps Engineer), Linda (Security Engineer)
2 million daily active users with 24-hour token expiry
5% of mobile users experiencing stuck sessions
15ms latency per validation due to database lookups
Database lookups for every token validation create exploitable bottleneck
Option 1: Redis Caching with Shorter TTL
Quickest to implement, maintains self-contained JWTs
Reduces latency to under 5ms, costs nothing extra
Downside: still requires client-side token refresh logic
Option 2: Opaque Tokens with Session Database
Every API call hits database - requires read replicas
Option 3: OAuth 2.0 with Third-Party Identity Provider
No maintenance, enterprise features, SOC2 compliant
Costs ~$2,000/month, gives up control, migration takes time
Need cache invalidation for revoke endpoint
Follow-up meeting scheduled in two weeks

action_items.task: Provision Redis cluster with 99.99% SLA
Implement token caching logic and handle key rotation
Update security documentation
Run penetration test after deployment

knowledge_points: Redis caching reduces JWT validation latency from 15ms to under 5ms
15-minute cache TTL provides balance between performance and security
Cache invalidation required for token revocation endpoint
Key rotation must be implemented without downtime
Option 1 allows future migration to OAuth 2.0 provider if needed
Database lookups for token validation create security vulnerabilities
Third-party auth providers (Auth0/Okta) offer strongest security but add cost and dependency
